prompt: Our flat ROLE_GRANTS table in permits.py has become copy-paste soup — editor repeats everything viewer has, admin repeats everything, and last month a new permission got added to three of the four roles and silently missed the fourth. I want a RoleGraph class in the same module for the next-gen checks (the flat functions stay, half the codebase still calls them). Shape: g.add_role(name, grants=(), denies=(), inherits=()) where parents must already be defined — reference an unknown parent and you get a KeyError, redefine a role and you get a ValueError, blank names ValueError. A role's permissions flow down from everything it inherits, transitively, diamonds fine. denies= is the important part, it's why we're doing this: contractors get editor minus the risky stuff. Deny-overrides semantics, the same rule Windows ACLs use: if ANY role in the resolved closure denies a permission, the answer is no — even if something else grants it, even if a child role tries to re-grant it further down. Query with g.allowed(role_names, perm) (empty list of roles: False; unknown role: KeyError), g.effective_grants(role) returning the net frozenset, and g.require(roles, perm) raising the existing PermissionDenied. Acceptance tests live in test_permits.py — python3 test_permits.py — and they also re-check all the flat-function behavior, which must not change. Don't modify the tests.
